Professor View 01 / 35
Business + Legal English -- 48 Aulas

Simone Quiles de Santana Marques

De "sobreviver em reuniões internacionais" a negociar contratos com confiança em inglês

A2-B1 Advogada Corporativa Contratos e M&A na Telefônica 60 min / Online
Progresso Geral0%
Speed:
Simone Quiles de Santana Marques
50 anos
Advogada Corporativa -- Contratos e M&A na Telefônica
Não informada
A2-B1 (Pré-intermediário a Intermediário)
Business + Legal English 70% / General 30%
48 aulas de 60 min
Online -- 2x/semana

Jornada de Transformação

De: Advogada que se sente "mais pressionada no trabalho" porque o inglês "começou a fazer falta" -- eternamente intermediária

Para: Profissional que conduz reuniões corporativas internacionais e negocia contratos em inglês com confiança real

Promessa do Programa

"De 'Forever Intermediate' travada nas reuniões da Telefônica a advogada que conduz contratos e M&A em inglês com confiança -- em 48 aulas, sem começar e parar de novo."

Forças

  • Autoconsciência pedagógica elevada
  • Vocabulário técnico-corporativo robusto
  • Motivação intrínseca e urgência real
  • Hábito auditivo estabelecido
  • Pragmatismo e comprometimento

Pontos de Melhoria

  • Padrão histórico de descontinuidade
  • Resistência à exposição oral
  • Baixo consumo de input em inglês
  • Perfeccionismo que bloqueia produção oral

Currículo -- 48 Aulas

#TemaFoco LinguísticoAtividade PrincipalHomework
1Who Is Simone? -- Diagnostic + Self-IntroductionPresent simple, professional vocabularyProfessional Identity Card + diagnosticRecord 90-sec self-introduction
2My Daily Routine -- Work at TelefonicaPresent simple: affirm/neg/questionsDaily routine descriptionListen to BEP + write 3 phrases
3Email Essentials -- Corporate EmailsEmail structures: opening/body/closingRead and respond to emailWrite reply to fictional email
4Phone and Video CallsPhone phrases: opening/clarifying/closingSimulated conference callRecord voicemail in English
5Small Talk That WorksQuestion forms, conversation startersNetworking role-playWatch TED Talk + note phrases
6Meeting BasicsModal verbs: can/could/shouldMeeting participation simulationSummarize meeting in 5 sentences
7Expressing OpinionsOpinion phrases: I think, In my viewDebate simulationWrite opinion paragraph
8Giving UpdatesPresent continuous + time expressionsTeam update presentationRecord 2-min update
9Asking Smart QuestionsQuestion words + indirect questionsQ&A session role-playPrepare 5 meeting questions
10Travel English ITravel vocabulary, Could I requestsAirport/hotel role-playListen to travel podcast
11Contract VocabularyLegal nouns: clause, liability, breachContract reading exerciseCreate legal glossary
12Reading ContractsConditional: if/unless/providedAnalyze sample clauseIdentify 5 key terms
13Negotiation BasicsNegotiation phrasesNegotiation opening role-playWrite negotiation email
14Due DiligenceRisk vocabulary: assess, mitigateDue diligence discussionSummarize risk findings
15Cultural Communication -- UK and GermanyCultural comparison vocabularyCross-cultural simulationResearch 3 differences
16Leading a MeetingMeeting management phrasesChair a simulated meetingWrite meeting agenda
17Presenting DataNumbers, percentages, comparisonsData presentation role-playPresent 3 charts
18Stakeholder UpdatesReporting structures, formal registerExecutive briefing simulationWrite update email
19Handling ObjectionsConcession phrases: however, neverthelessObjection handling role-playRecord response to objections
20Social EnglishSocial vocabulary, advanced small talkCorporate dinner role-playWatch Suits + note vocab
21M&A VocabularyM&A terms: acquire, merge, divestM&A case study discussionCreate M&A vocab cards
22Contract NegotiationConditional + must/shall/mayFull negotiation role-playDraft 3 contract clauses
23Regulatory LanguageCompliance vocabularyRegulatory discussionRead compliance article
24Risk AssessmentRisk matrix vocabularyRisk assessment presentationWrite risk summary
25Cross-Border TransactionsInternational legal vocabularyMulti-party deal simulationResearch case study
26Persuasion SkillsPersuasive structures, connectorsPersuasive presentationRecord 3-min argument
27Difficult ConversationsSoftening language, diplomatic phrasesBad news delivery role-playWrite diplomatic email
28Multi-Party CallsTurn-taking, summarizing4-person call simulationSummarize call in writing
29Presentation SkillsPresentation frameworkFull 10-min presentationCreate slides outline
30Travel English IIConference vocabulary, networkingConference simulationListen to HBR podcast
31Drafting in EnglishLegal writing: shall, herebyDraft contract sectionReview sample clause
32Legal CorrespondenceFormal register, legal letter structureWrite formal legal letterRespond to legal inquiry
33Board MeetingsBoard vocab: resolution, quorumBoard meeting simulationWrite meeting minutes
34Dispute ResolutionADR vocab: mediate, arbitrateMediation role-playSummarize resolution options
35European Business CultureCultural intelligence vocabularyCross-cultural negotiationPrepare cultural briefing
36Simulation -- M&A MeetingAll M&A + meeting structures30-min M&A immersionReflect on performance
37Simulation -- Contract NegotiationAll legal + negotiation30-min negotiationRecord highlights
38Simulation -- Stakeholder PresentationAll presentation + data20-min executive presentationWrite follow-up email
39Simulation -- Conference CallAll communication structures25-min multi-party callSummarize action items
40Mid-Program AssessmentReview all structuresAssessment activitiesSelf-evaluation journal
41Advanced NegotiationAdvanced tacticsHigh-pressure simulationAnalyze strategies
42Legal OpinionsOpinion structures, hedgingLegal opinion presentationWrite opinion summary
43Crisis CommunicationUrgency vocabulary, action languageCrisis response simulationDraft crisis email
44Executive BriefingsExecutive vocabulary, concise reportingC-suite briefing role-playPrepare executive summary
45International NetworkingAdvanced networking, follow-upNetworking event simulationWrite 3 follow-up messages
46Simulation -- Board MeetingAll structures integrated35-min board immersionFinal self-assessment
47Simulation -- M&A ClosingAll structures integrated35-min deal closingRecord reflection audio
48Graduation -- Final AssessmentAll structures reviewFinal performance + celebrationContinuation plan

Welcome, Simone!

Onboarding

Welcome to the program! Before each class, complete the Pre-class activities. Here are 5 emergency phrases you can use at any time:

1 Could you repeat that, please?
2 I am not sure I understand. Could you explain?
3 Let me think about that for a moment.
4 Could I ask a question?
5 In my experience, I would say...
Lesson 01 -- Pre-class

Who Is Simone?

Diagnostic + Self-Introduction: Present Simple
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Learn the key words for this lesson. Click Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Contract -- a formal written legal document between two or more parties
"Simone works on international contracts at Telefonica."
Negotiate -- to discuss terms in order to reach an agreement
"We need to negotiate the terms of this agreement."
Agreement -- an arrangement accepted by all parties involved
"Both parties signed the agreement last week."
Corporate -- related to a large company or business group
"She handles corporate legal matters for the company."
Responsible -- having the duty to manage or take care of something
"I am responsible for reviewing all contracts."
Currently -- at the present time; right now
"We are currently working on a new international deal."
Deal -- to handle, manage, or be involved with something
"I deal with international clients every day."
Meeting -- a gathering of people for discussion or decisions
"The meeting with the London office is at three."
Colleague -- a person you work with in a professional setting
"My colleague from Germany will join the call."
Introduce -- to present yourself or someone else formally
"Let me introduce myself. I am Simone Marques."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition in English.

Contract
Negotiate
Agreement
Corporate
Responsible
Currently
Deal
Meeting
Colleague
Introduce

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Simone works at Telefonica in the legal department. She is a corporate lawyer. Every day, she reviews international contracts and negotiates terms with partners from other countries. She deals with colleagues from the United Kingdom and Germany. Simone is responsible for contracts and mergers and acquisitions. She currently handles a new corporate agreement. Her colleague from the London office joins meetings every week. Simone introduces herself at every international meeting.

1. What does Simone do every day?
A She introduces herself at meetings.
B She reviews international contracts and negotiates terms with partners.
C She handles a new corporate agreement.
2. Which countries does Simone deal with?
A France and Spain.
B The United Kingdom and Germany.
C The United States and Japan.
3. What is Simone responsible for?
A Managing the London office.
B Training new colleagues.
C Contracts and mergers and acquisitions.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Present Simple = facts, habits, routines (things that happen regularly or are always true)

FormStructureExample
AffirmativeSubject + verb (base/s)"She works at Telefonica."
NegativeSubject + do/does + not + verb"She does not work on weekends."
QuestionDo/Does + subject + verb?"Does she work with international partners?"

Common Mistake:

"She work at Telefonica."

"She works at Telefonica."

With he/she/it, add -s or -es to the verb. In questions: "Does she work?" (no -s on the verb).

"Does she works with partners?"

"Does she work with partners?"

After "does", the verb goes back to base form (no -s).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word.

"Simone at Telefonica in the legal department."
"She contracts with international partners."
"He is for the London office operations."
"They deal with a new corporate agreement."
"We ourselves at every meeting."
"My and I negotiate deals together."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the sentences in the correct order.

? "I am responsible for contracts and mergers and acquisitions."
? "I negotiate terms with partners from the United Kingdom and Germany."
? "Good morning. My name is Simone Marques."
? "I currently deal with international agreements."
? "I am a corporate lawyer at Telefonica."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen, record, and compare your pronunciation.

Good morning. My name is Simone Marques.
I am a corporate lawyer at Telefonica.
I am responsible for contracts and mergers and acquisitions.
I currently deal with international agreements.
I negotiate terms with partners from the United Kingdom and Germany.

Stage 4: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are at a Telefonica international meeting. How would you introduce yourself? Record your answer in English. Try to include: your name, your role, what you are responsible for, and who you work with.

Survival Card -- Lesson 1

1 Good morning. My name is Simone Marques.
2 I am a corporate lawyer at Telefonica.
3 I am responsible for contracts and mergers and acquisitions.
4 I currently deal with international agreements.
5 I negotiate terms with partners from the United Kingdom and Germany.
Lesson 02 -- Pre-class

My Daily Routine

Describing Work at Telefônica: Present Simple + Adverbs of Frequency
0%
▼

Stage 2.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Learn the key words for this lesson. Click Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Schedule -- a plan showing times for tasks or events
"I check my schedule every morning before work."
Review -- to examine something carefully
"Simone reviews contracts carefully before signing."
Deadline -- the latest time to complete something
"The deadline for the report is next Friday."
Report -- a written document with information
"I need to send a report to the London office."
Attend -- to be present at a meeting or event
"She attends three meetings every day."
Approve -- to officially agree to something
"The manager approved the new contract yesterday."
Draft -- a first version of a document
"I am drafting a new agreement for the client."
Department -- a section of a company
"The legal department has fifteen lawyers."
Brief -- to give someone important information
"I need to brief my team before the call."
Coordinate -- to organize people to work together
"We coordinate with offices in three countries."

Stage 2.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition in English.

Schedule
Review
Deadline
Report
Attend
Approve
Draft
Department
Brief
Coordinate

Stage 2.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Simone usually starts her day at eight thirty. She always checks her emails first. She often reviews contracts in the morning. She sometimes attends four meetings in one day, but she rarely attends more than that. She never misses a deadline. Her team usually coordinates with the London office in the afternoon. On Fridays, Simone always briefs her department about the week's progress.

1. How often does Simone miss a deadline?
A Always
B Sometimes
C Never
2. When does Simone usually review contracts?
A In the afternoon
B In the morning
C On weekends
3. What does Simone always do on Fridays?
A Attend four meetings
B Brief her department
C Draft new contracts

Stage 2.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Adverbs of Frequency = words that describe how often something happens. They go BEFORE the main verb (but AFTER "to be").

FrequencyAdverbExample
100%Always"She always checks her emails."
80%Usually"I usually start at eight thirty."
60%Often"We often coordinate with London."
40%Sometimes"She sometimes attends four meetings."
10%Rarely"He rarely misses a deadline."
0%Never"I never leave without checking."

Position rule: Subject + adverb + verb / Subject + be + adverb

Common Mistake:

"She reviews always her emails."

"She always reviews her emails."

Position rule: Subject + adverb + verb. With "to be": Subject + be + adverb.

Stage 2.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word.

"Simone reviews her emails in the morning."
"She attends three meetings a day."
"The for the contract is next Monday."
"I need to with the Munich office today."
"He all the agreements for the department."
"We miss a deadline for important reports."

Stage 2.6: Put in Order

Order

Put the sentences in the correct order.

? "In the afternoon, I draft contracts and review documents."
? "On Fridays, I brief my department about the week's progress."
? "I usually arrive at eight thirty and check my emails."
? "Before I leave, I always check tomorrow's deadlines."
? "Then, I review my schedule and attend morning meetings."

Stage 2.7: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen, record, and compare your pronunciation.

I usually start my day at eight thirty.
I review contracts and attend meetings every day.
I always brief my team before important calls.
I coordinate with offices in London and Munich.
I never miss a deadline for contract reviews.

Stage 2.8: Think About It

Reflection
Describe your typical work day at Telefônica from morning to evening. What do you always do? What do you usually do? What do you never do?

Survival Card -- Lesson 2

1 What time does the meeting start?
2 I usually review contracts in the morning.
3 Could you send me the report by Friday?
4 I need to coordinate with the London office.
5 Let me check my schedule.
Aula 03 -- Pre-class

Email Essentials -- Opening, Body & Closing

How to write clear, professional corporate emails: open with a polite greeting and reason, keep the body short, and close with a clear next step. New vocabulary: recipient, subject line, attachment, greeting, regarding, forward, reply, CC, sign off, follow up.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Recipient -- the person an email is sent to
"The recipient is the person who receives your email." (Destinatário)
Subject line -- the short title that shows the topic of the email
"A clear subject line tells the reader the topic at once." (Assunto)
Attachment -- a file you send together with an email
"Please find the contract as an attachment." (Anexo)
Greeting -- the polite opening line of an email
"A formal greeting is Dear Mr. Reed." (Saudação)
Regarding -- about; concerning a topic
"I am writing regarding the agreement." (A respeito de)
Forward -- to send a received email on to another person
"Could you forward this email to your partner?" (Encaminhar)
Reply -- to write an answer to an email
"I will reply to your message this afternoon." (Responder)
CC -- to send a copy of an email to other people
"Please put my manager in CC." (Com cópia)
Sign off -- to end an email with a polite closing
"I sign off with Best regards." (Despedir-se)
Follow up -- to send a reminder or check progress later
"I will follow up on Friday if I hear nothing." (Dar seguimento)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Recipient
Subject line
Attachment
Greeting
Regarding
Forward
Reply
CC
Sign off
Follow up

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

On Tuesday, Thomas Reed sent Simone an email regarding the NDA. The subject line was clear, and the greeting was polite: "Dear Ms. Quiles." He sent the draft as an attachment and asked her to reply with her comments. Simone decided to forward the email to her contracts team and put her manager in CC, so everyone stayed informed. In her answer she kept the body short, asked one clear question, and remembered to sign off politely with "Best regards." She promised to follow up on Friday if she did not hear back. The recipient, Thomas, answered within an hour.

1. Why was the email easy to understand?
A The subject line was clear and the greeting was polite.
B It had no subject line.
C It was written in capital letters.
2. What did Simone do so her team stayed informed?
A She deleted the email.
B She forwarded the email and put her manager in CC.
C She printed the contract.
3. How did Simone close her email?
A With no closing at all.
B "See you, bye."
C She signed off politely with "Best regards."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Email Code

GRAMMAR

Num e-mail profissional, siga três partes: abra, escreva o corpo e encerre. / In a professional email, follow three parts: open, write the body, and close.

Part / ParteWhat you do / O que fazerExample / Exemplo
Opening / AberturaSaudação + motivo / Greeting + reason"Dear Mr. Reed, I am writing regarding the NDA."
Body / CorpoUm pedido claro; cite o anexo / One clear request; mention the attachment"Could you please review the attached draft?"
Closing / EncerramentoPróximo passo + despedida / Next step + sign off"I look forward to your reply. Best regards, Simone."
Key point / Ponto-chaveComece com uma saudação formal e o motivo, faça um pedido claro no corpo e encerre com o próximo passo antes de se despedir.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I am writing the agreement."
"Please find the contract as an ."
"Could you this email to your partner?"
"I will to your message this afternoon."
"A clear tells the reader the topic."
"I will on Friday if I hear nothing."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?Simone reads the clear subject line and the polite greeting.
?Thomas sends Simone an email with the NDA draft as an attachment.
?Thomas follows up on Thursday, and they sign off.
?Simone replies and asks Thomas to review two clauses.
?Simone forwards the email to her team and adds a colleague in CC.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Dear Mr. Reed, I am writing regarding the NDA.
Prezado Sr. Reed, escrevo a respeito do acordo de confidencialidade.
Could you please review the attached draft?
Você poderia revisar a minuta em anexo, por favor?
Please put my manager in CC and forward it to the team.
Por favor, coloque meu gestor em cópia e encaminhe para a equipe.
I look forward to your reply. Best regards.
Aguardo sua resposta. Atenciosamente.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

You start a formal email to external counsel. How do you open?
A "Hey, what's up?"
B "Dear Mr. Reed, I am writing regarding the NDA."
C "Read this now."
You mention a file in your email. What must you remember to do?
A Delete the subject line.
B Add the file as an attachment before you send it.
C Write in capital letters.
You want your manager to see an email you send to a client. What do you do?
A Tell no one.
B Put your manager in CC.
C Send a separate fax.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think about an email you often send at work, perhaps to a counterpart on a contract. Record a short email out loud: open it (greeting and reason), write one clear request in the body (mention an attachment), and close it with a polite sign off and a next step. Use at least four new words from today.

Survival Card -- Lesson 3

1Dear Mr. Reed, I am writing regarding the NDA.Prezado Sr. Reed, escrevo a respeito do acordo.
2Please find the signed draft as an attachment.Segue a minuta assinada em anexo.
3Could you reply by Friday and put me in CC?Você pode responder até sexta e me colocar em cópia?
4I'll forward your email to the contracts team.Vou encaminhar seu e-mail para a equipe de contratos.
5I look forward to your reply. Best regards.Aguardo sua resposta. Atenciosamente.
Aula 04 -- Pre-class

Phone & Video Calls -- Opening, Clarifying & Closing

How to handle professional phone and video calls: open, clarify, and close. New vocabulary: voicemail, hold on, reach, available, connection, mute, speak up, call back, hang up, clarify. Functional phrases for the three phases of a call.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Voicemail -- a recorded message left when no one answers
"I left a voicemail when she did not pick up." (Correio de voz)
Hold on -- to wait for a short time on the phone
"Could you hold on while I open the contract?" (Aguardar)
Reach -- to succeed in contacting someone
"I tried to reach you all morning." (Contatar)
Available -- free and able to talk or meet
"Is Mr. Pearson available right now?" (Disponível)
Connection -- the signal quality during a call
"Sorry, the connection is very weak today." (Conexão)
Mute -- to turn your microphone off
"Please mute your mic when you are not speaking." (Silenciar)
Speak up -- to talk more loudly
"Could you speak up? I can barely hear you." (Falar mais alto)
Call back -- to return someone's phone call
"I will call you back after the meeting." (Retornar a ligação)
Hang up -- to end a phone or video call
"Let us not hang up before we confirm the date." (Desligar)
Clarify -- to make something clearer
"Could you clarify the last clause for me?" (Esclarecer)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Voicemail
Hold on
Reach
Available
Connection
Mute
Speak up
Call back
Hang up
Clarify

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

On Monday, Mark tried to reach Simone three times, but she was in court and not available, so he left a voicemail. When Simone heard it, she decided to call him back on a video call. At the start, the connection was weak, so Mark asked her to speak up and to clarify the indemnity clause. Simone opened the call clearly: "Hi Mark, this is Simone speaking. I'm calling about the agreement." She asked him to hold on while she opened the file, and she reminded a noisy colleague to mute his microphone. Before they could hang up, they confirmed the next step and agreed to talk again on Friday.

1. Why did Mark leave a voicemail?
A Simone was in court and not available.
B The agreement was already signed.
C He wanted to mute the call.
2. What did Mark ask Simone to do when the connection was weak?
A Hang up immediately.
B Speak up and clarify the clause.
C Send the contract by mail.
3. How does Simone open the call professionally?
A "Who is this?"
B "Hi Mark, this is Simone speaking. I'm calling about the agreement."
C "What do you want?"

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Phone Code

GRAMMAR

Numa ligação profissional, siga três passos: abra, esclareça e encerre. / On a professional call, follow three steps: open, clarify, and close.

Phase / FaseWhen / QuandoExample / Exemplo
Opening / AberturaIdentifique-se e diga o motivo / Identify yourself and your reason"Hi, this is Simone speaking. I'm calling about the agreement."
Clarifying / EsclarecerConfirme que entendeu; resolva a linha ruim / Check you understood; fix a bad line"Sorry, could you speak up? Could you clarify that?"
Closing / EncerrarConfirme o próximo passo antes de desligar / Confirm the next step before you hang up"Thanks for your time. I'll call you back."
Key point / Ponto-chaveSempre se identifique primeiro, confirme que entendeu e encerre com o próximo passo antes de desligar.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I tried to you all morning."
"Is Mr. Pearson right now?"
"Sorry, the is very weak today."
"Could you the last clause for me?"
"I will call you after the meeting."
"Please your mic when you are not speaking."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?Simone calls Mark back and opens the video call.
?Mark cannot reach Simone, so he leaves a voicemail.
?Simone closes the call and leaves a voicemail update for her team.
?The connection is weak, so they speak up and clarify the open points.
?They agree on the indemnity number and the new timeline.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Hi Mark, this is Simone speaking. I'm calling about the agreement.
Oi Mark, aqui é a Simone. Estou ligando sobre o contrato.
Sorry, could you speak up? The connection is weak.
Desculpe, você pode falar mais alto? A conexão está ruim.
Could you clarify the indemnity clause for me?
Você poderia esclarecer a cláusula de indenização para mim?
Thanks for your time. I'll call you back this afternoon.
Obrigada pelo seu tempo. Eu retorno a ligação à tarde.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

The line is bad and you cannot hear the other person well. What do you say?
A "Speak!"
B "Sorry, could you speak up? The connection is weak."
C "I will hang up now."
You call a client but they do not answer. What is the professional thing to do?
A Call ten more times in a row.
B Leave a clear voicemail and ask them to call back.
C Do nothing and wait.
You need to end the call, but you have not agreed on the next step. What do you do?
A Hang up immediately.
B Confirm the next step before you hang up.
C Mute and leave the call.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think about an important phone or video call you have at work, perhaps with a counterpart on a contract. Record a short call: open it (say who you are and why you are calling), clarify one difficult point, and close it clearly with the next step. Use at least four new words from today.

Survival Card -- Lesson 4

1Hi, this is Simone speaking. I'm calling about the agreement.Oi, aqui é a Simone. Estou ligando sobre o contrato.
2Sorry, could you speak up? The connection is weak.Desculpe, pode falar mais alto? A conexão está ruim.
3Could you clarify that point for me?Você poderia esclarecer esse ponto para mim?
4Can I call you back in ten minutes?Posso retornar a ligação em dez minutos?
5Thanks for your time. Let's confirm the next step.Obrigada pelo seu tempo. Vamos confirmar o próximo passo.
Aula 05 -- Pre-class

Small Talk That Works -- Conversation Starters & Question Forms

How to start, build, and keep a professional conversation when networking. New vocabulary: small talk, break the ice, get to know, catch up, in common, bring up, keep in touch, networking, conversation starter, wrap up. Open vs closed questions and correct question word order.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each expression and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Small talk -- light, casual conversation
"We made some small talk before the meeting started." (Conversa informal)
Break the ice -- to start a conversation in a relaxed way
"A simple question is a great way to break the ice." (Quebrar o gelo)
Get to know -- to become familiar with someone
"I would love to get to know your team better." (Conhecer)
Catch up -- to exchange recent news with someone
"Let us catch up over lunch and talk more." (Atualizar a conversa)
In common -- shared between people
"It turns out we have a lot in common." (Em comum)
Bring up -- to start talking about a topic
"She brought up an interesting point in the panel." (Mencionar)
Keep in touch -- to stay in contact with someone
"Let us keep in touch after the conference." (Manter contato)
Networking -- meeting people to build work relationships
"There is a networking lunch tomorrow at one." (Fazer contatos)
Conversation starter -- a topic or question to begin a chat
"Asking about the trip is a good conversation starter." (Assunto para iniciar conversa)
Wrap up -- to bring something to a close
"Let us wrap up and exchange contacts." (Encerrar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each expression with its translation.

Small talk
Break the ice
Get to know
Catch up
In common
Bring up
Keep in touch
Networking
Conversation starter
Wrap up

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At the legal forum, Simone wanted to do some networking, but she felt nervous. Instead of talking about work right away, she used a simple question as a conversation starter to break the ice. She met Daniel and they quickly found they had a lot in common. Before she brought up business, they took a moment to get to know each other. When the chat was going well, she asked to catch up over lunch, and they agreed to keep in touch. At the end of the day, she helped wrap up the event by sharing her contacts with the team.

1. Why did Simone feel nervous at first?
A She wanted to do some networking but felt nervous.
B The event was cancelled.
C She lost her contract.
2. How did Simone break the ice?
A By talking about work immediately.
B With a simple question as a conversation starter.
C By leaving the event early.
3. What did Simone and Daniel agree to do?
A Cancel the panel.
B Catch up over lunch and keep in touch.
C Stop talking to each other.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Open vs Closed Questions

GRAMMAR

Para manter uma conversa, use perguntas abertas; comece com What, Where, How. / To keep a conversation going, use open questions; start with What, Where, How.

Type / TipoStarts with / Comeca comExample / Exemplo
Open / AbertaWhat, Where, How, Why, How long"What do you do?" / "Where are you from?"
Closed / FechadaDo, Did, Are, Is, Have"Do you know many people here?"
Word order / OrdemAuxiliary + subject + verb"What do you do?" (nao "What you do?")
Key point / Ponto-chavePerguntas abertas mantem a conversa viva; sempre faca uma pergunta de acompanhamento.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"A good way to break the is to ask a simple question."
"It turns out we have a lot in ."
"Let us up over lunch and talk more."
"We should keep in after the event."
"She up an interesting point in the panel."
"There is a lunch tomorrow at one."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?They find they have a lot in common and talk about their work.
?Simone arrives at the legal conference and feels nervous about networking.
?They exchange numbers and promise to keep in touch.
?Daniel breaks the ice with a friendly question at the coffee break.
?Simone brings up a point from the panel, and they agree to catch up at lunch.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Hi, I don't think we have met. I'm Simone.
Oi, acho que não nos conhecemos. Eu sou a Simone.
What brings you to the conference?
O que te traz à conferência?
It looks like we have a lot in common.
Parece que temos muito em comum.
Let's keep in touch after the event.
Vamos manter contato depois do evento.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

You meet someone new at a legal event and want to break the ice. What do you say?
A "What is your job?"
B "Hi, I don't think we have met. I'm Simone. Is this your first time here?"
C "Who are you?"
You want to keep the conversation going with an open question. What do you ask?
A "What brings you to the conference?"
B "Do you like it here?"
C "Is it raining?"
The conversation is ending and you want to stay connected. What do you do?
A Walk away without a word.
B Suggest catching up and offer to keep in touch.
C Ask for nothing and leave.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are at a legal conference and you meet a lawyer you have never met. Record a short conversation: break the ice, ask two open questions to get to know them, find something in common, and close by agreeing to keep in touch. Use at least four new expressions from today.

Survival Card -- Lesson 5

1Hi, I don't think we have met. I'm Simone.Oi, acho que não nos conhecemos. Eu sou a Simone.
2What brings you to the conference?O que te traz à conferência?
3It looks like we have a lot in common.Parece que temos muito em comum.
4Would you like to catch up over lunch?Você gostaria de atualizar a conversa no almoço?
5Let's keep in touch. Here is my card.Vamos manter contato. Aqui está meu cartão.
Aula 06 -- Pre-class

Meeting Basics -- Modal Verbs for Meeting Participation

How to take part in a meeting with the right words and tone. New vocabulary: agenda, minutes, chair, raise a point, interrupt, recap, action item, postpone, move on, show of hands. Modal verbs can, could, and should for requests, suggestions, and advice.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Agenda -- the list of topics planned for a meeting
"There are three items on today's agenda." (Pauta)
Minutes -- the written record of a meeting
"Could you take the minutes today?" (Ata)
Chair -- to lead and guide a meeting
"Daniel will chair the meeting on Thursday." (Presidir)
Raise a point -- to bring up a topic to discuss
"Can I raise a point before we move on?" (Levantar um ponto)
Interrupt -- to stop someone while they are speaking
"Sorry to interrupt, but can I add something?" (Interromper)
Recap -- to summarize the main points again
"Could you recap the main points before we close?" (Recapitular)
Action item -- a task someone must do after the meeting
"Let's note that as an action item." (Item de acao)
Postpone -- to move a meeting to a later time
"We should postpone the last item to next week." (Adiar)
Move on -- to go to the next topic
"Could we move on to the next item?" (Passar adiante)
Show of hands -- a quick vote by raising hands
"Let's have a show of hands on the new deadline." (Votacao com as maos)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Agenda
Minutes
Chair
Raise a point
Interrupt
Recap
Action item
Postpone
Move on
Show of hands

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Daniel will chair the project meeting on Thursday, and he sent the agenda in advance. There are three items on it. At the start, Simone says, "Can I raise a point about the deadline?" Daniel replies, "Of course. Could you recap where we are with the contract?" After a short discussion, they agree they should postpone the last item to next week. Daniel asks Simone to take the minutes, and they note two action items. Finally, Simone asks, "Should we move on to the next item?"

1. Who will chair the meeting?
A Daniel will chair the meeting.
B No one is in charge.
C The client chairs it.
2. What do they decide to do with the last item?
A Cancel it completely.
B Postpone it to next week.
C Discuss it twice.
3. What does Daniel ask Simone to do?
A Leave the meeting early.
B Chair the next meeting.
C Recap the contract and take the minutes.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Can, Could & Should

GRAMMAR

Em reunioes, escolha o modal certo: can (informal), could (mais educado), should (conselho). Depois do modal, use o verbo na base, sem to e sem s. / In meetings, choose the right modal: can (informal), could (more polite), should (advice). After the modal, use the base verb, with no to and no -s.

ModalUse / UsoExample / Exemplo
canPermission / ability (informal) -- permissao"Can I raise a point?"
couldPolite request / suggestion -- pedido educado"Could you recap the agenda?"
shouldAdvice / recommendation -- conselho"We should postpone this item."
Regra de ouromodal + verbo base (sem "to", sem "-s"): "She should send" (nao "should sends").

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"There are three items on today's ."
"Could you take the today?"
"Can I a point before we move on?"
"We should the last item to next week."
"Could you the main points before we close?"
"Should we on to the next item?"

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?The team discusses the new deadline and agrees on a plan.
?Daniel opens the meeting and shares the agenda.
?Simone takes the minutes and sends the action items to everyone.
?Simone raises the point about the cross-border contract first.
?They run out of time, so they postpone the last item to next week.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Can I raise a point?
Posso levantar um ponto?
Could you recap the main points?
Você poderia recapitular os pontos principais?
We should postpone this to next week.
Devemos adiar isto para a próxima semana.
Could you take the minutes today?
Você poderia fazer a ata hoje?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each meeting situation.

You want to add a topic to the discussion. What do you say?
A "I talk now."
B "Can I raise a point?"
C "You must listen me."
You did not follow the last part and want a short summary. What do you ask?
A "Could you recap the main points?"
B "Say all again now."
C "I no understand."
There is no time for the last item. What do you suggest?
A "We should cancels it."
B "Could you to postpone?"
C "We should postpone this to next week."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are chairing a short project meeting. Record yourself: open the meeting with the agenda, invite the team to raise a point, ask someone to recap, suggest postponing one item, and assign one action item. Use can, could, and should at least once each.

Survival Card -- Lesson 6

1Can I raise a point?Posso levantar um ponto?
2Could you recap the main points?Você poderia recapitular os pontos principais?
3We should postpone this to next week.Devemos adiar isto para a próxima semana.
4Could you take the minutes today?Você poderia fazer a ata hoje?
5Let's move on to the next item.Vamos passar para o próximo item.
Aula 07 -- Pre-class

Expressing Opinions -- Opinion Phrases for Debates

How to share your opinion and defend it in a discussion. New vocabulary: opinion, point of view, agree, disagree, persuade, convince, argument, emphasize, stance, common ground. Opinion phrases I think, in my view, I agree, and I disagree for debates.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Opinion -- what a person thinks about something
"What is your opinion on the new timeline?" (Opinião)
Point of view -- the way a person sees a situation
"From my point of view, quality comes first." (Ponto de vista)
Agree -- to have the same opinion
"I agree with your point of view." (Concordar)
Disagree -- to have a different opinion
"I disagree, because the deadline is too tight." (Discordar)
Persuade -- to make someone want to do something
"We need to persuade the client with a clear plan." (Persuadir)
Convince -- to make someone believe something is true
"Good reasons will convince the team." (Convencer)
Argument -- a reason that supports an opinion
"My main argument is that quality matters most." (Argumento)
Emphasize -- to give a point special importance
"I want to emphasize one key point." (Enfatizar)
Stance -- a clear position on a topic
"We should present one united stance." (Posição)
Common ground -- an idea two sides both agree on
"We found common ground on a new plan." (Base comum)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Opinion
Point of view
Agree
Disagree
Persuade
Convince
Argument
Emphasize
Stance
Common ground

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Daniel and Simone disagree about the client proposal. Daniel says, "In my view, the timeline is too tight." Simone replies, "I agree with your point of view. I think we should ask for more time." Daniel adds his main argument: quality matters more than speed. Simone says, "I see your point, but we also need to persuade the client, not pressure them." After a short debate, they find common ground and decide to present one united stance to the client.

1. What is Daniel's view on the timeline?
A In his view, it is too tight.
B He thinks it is perfect.
C He has no opinion.
2. How does Simone disagree with one point?
A She says nothing.
B She says "I see your point, but..." politely.
C She leaves the meeting.
3. How does the debate end?
A They never agree.
B They cancel the contract.
C They find common ground and a united stance.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Opinion Phrases

GRAMMAR

Para dar opiniao, use I think (neutro) ou In my view (mais formal), seguidos de uma ideia completa (sujeito + verbo). Para reagir, use I agree / I disagree; para discordar com educacao, I see your point, but... Lembre: agree e verbo, entao nunca diga "I am agree". / To give an opinion, use I think (neutral) or In my view (more formal) plus a full idea (subject + verb). To react, use I agree / I disagree; to disagree politely, I see your point, but... Remember: agree is a verb, so never say "I am agree".

PhraseUse / UsoExample / Exemplo
I think ...Give your opinion (neutral) -- opiniao neutra"I think we should wait."
In my view, ...Give your opinion (formal) -- opiniao formal"In my view, the risk is high."
I agree / I disagreeReact to an opinion -- reagir"I agree with you."
Regra de ourofrase de opiniao + ideia completa (sujeito + verbo). Nunca "I am agree" -- diga "I agree".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I we should ask for more time."
"In my , quality matters most."
"I with your point of view."
"My main is that quality matters most."
"We need to the client with a clear plan."
"Let's find some common before we decide."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?They meet and compare their opinions about the timeline.
?Daniel shares his point of view in a voice note before the discussion.
?Simone writes a recap and sends one united stance to the client.
?Simone reads the discussion brief and prepares her own stance.
?After a short debate, they find common ground on a new plan.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I think we should ask for more time.
Eu acho que deveríamos pedir mais tempo.
In my view, quality matters most.
Na minha opinião, a qualidade importa mais.
I agree with your point of view.
Eu concordo com o seu ponto de vista.
I see your point, but I am not sure.
Entendo o seu ponto, mas não tenho certeza.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each discussion situation.

You want to give your opinion about the deadline. What do you say?
A "The deadline bad."
B "I think the deadline is too tight."
C "You must change it."
A colleague shares an idea and you have the same opinion. What do you say?
A "I agree with your point of view."
B "I am agree with you."
C "Yes the same."
You have a different opinion but want to stay polite. What do you say?
A "You are wrong."
B "I no agree."
C "I see your point, but I am not sure."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are in a discussion about a work decision. Record yourself: give your opinion with I think or in my view, support it with one argument, react to an imagined colleague with I agree or I see your point, but..., and suggest finding common ground.

Survival Card -- Lesson 7

1I think we should ask for more time.Eu acho que deveríamos pedir mais tempo.
2In my view, quality matters most.Na minha opinião, a qualidade importa mais.
3I agree with your point of view.Eu concordo com o seu ponto de vista.
4I see your point, but I am not sure.Entendo o seu ponto, mas não tenho certeza.
5Let's find common ground.Vamos encontrar uma base comum.
Aula 08 -- Pre-class

Giving Updates -- Present Continuous & Time Expressions

How to give a clear project update and report what is happening now. New vocabulary: progress, update, status, milestone, on track, delay, ongoing, phase, deliverable, pending. Grammar: the present continuous (am/is/are + verb-ing) with time expressions like right now and this week.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Progress -- movement forward toward a goal
"We are making good progress on the contract." (Progresso)
Update -- new information about how something is going
"I will give you a quick update on the merger." (Atualização)
Status -- how something is right now
"What is the status of the merger?" (Situação)
Milestone -- an important point you reach
"We reached an important milestone this week." (Marco)
On track -- going well and on time
"The project is on track for next month." (No prazo)
Delay -- when something happens later than planned
"There is a small delay in the tax review." (Atraso)
Ongoing -- still happening, not yet finished
"The negotiation is ongoing this week." (Em andamento)
Phase -- one stage of a project
"We are in the final phase of the deal." (Fase)
Deliverable -- a result you must produce and hand in
"The first deliverable is the draft agreement." (Entregável)
Pending -- still waiting for a decision or approval
"The approval is still pending." (Pendente)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Progress
Update
Status
Milestone
On track
Delay
Ongoing
Phase
Deliverable
Pending

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Daniel asks Simone for a quick update on the merger. Simone says, "Right now the team is reviewing the final contract, and we are making good progress." The project is on track, and this week they are reaching an important milestone. There is one small delay: the tax review is still ongoing, so that part is pending. At the moment the partners are checking the details, and they are signing next week. In the final phase, the team is preparing the signature meeting.

1. What is the team doing right now?
A They are reviewing the final contract.
B They are signing the contract.
C They are starting the project.
2. Why is one part pending?
A The contract is cancelled.
B The tax review is still ongoing, with a small delay.
C The team is on holiday.
3. What is happening in the final phase?
A They are stopping the deal.
B They are writing a new contract.
C They are preparing the signature meeting.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Continuous

GRAMMAR

Para dizer o que esta acontecendo agora, use o present continuous: am/is/are + verbo com -ing. Negativo: am/is/are + not + -ing. Pergunta: Am/Is/Are + sujeito + -ing. Use com expressoes de tempo como now, right now, at the moment, this week, these days. Cuidado: precisa das DUAS partes (be + -ing) -- nunca diga "I working" nem "I am work". / To say what is happening now, use the present continuous: am/is/are + verb-ing. Negative: am/is/are + not + verb-ing. Question: Am/Is/Are + subject + verb-ing. Use it with time expressions like now, right now, at the moment, this week, these days. Careful: you need BOTH parts (be + -ing).

Form / FormaStructure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Affirmativeam/is/are + verb-ing"I am working on the report."
Negativeam/is/are + not + verb-ing"We are not waiting yet."
QuestionAm/Is/Are + subject + verb-ing"Are you preparing the update?"
Time wordsnow, right now, at the moment, this week, these days

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We are making good on the deal."
"The project is on for next month."
"Right now the team reviewing the contract."
"At the moment I am the report."
"The approval is still ."
"We are in the final of the deal."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?They meet on a call and Simone shares each update.
?Daniel leaves a voice note asking for a project status update.
?Simone records a two-minute update and sends it to the team.
?Simone reads the project tracker and checks the latest progress.
?Simone explains the small delay and the pending approval.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Here is a quick update on the project.
Aqui está uma rápida atualização sobre o projeto.
Right now we are reviewing the contract.
Neste momento estamos revisando o contrato.
The project is on track.
O projeto está no prazo.
The approval is still pending.
A aprovação ainda está pendente.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each update situation.

Your manager asks how the contract is going right now. What do you say?
A "We review the contract."
B "We are reviewing the contract right now."
C "We reviewing the contract."
The deal is going well and on time. How do you report this?
A "The project is on track."
B "The project is delay."
C "The project pending."
The approval is not finished yet. How do you report it?
A "The approval is ready."
B "The approval finish."
C "The approval is still pending."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine your manager asks for a project update. Record yourself: report the status with the present continuous (what the team is doing right now), mention your progress and one milestone, explain a delay or a pending item, and describe the next phase.

Survival Card -- Lesson 8

1Here is a quick update on the project.Aqui está uma rápida atualização sobre o projeto.
2Right now we are reviewing the contract.Neste momento estamos revisando o contrato.
3The project is on track.O projeto está no prazo.
4The approval is still pending.A aprovação ainda está pendente.
5We are in the final phase.Estamos na fase final.
Aula 09 -- Pre-class

Asking Smart Questions -- Question Words & Indirect Questions

How to ask smart, polite questions to get the information you need. New vocabulary: inquire, wonder, specify, rephrase, confirm, assumption, concern, detail, relevant, probe. Grammar: indirect questions (Could you tell me..., Do you know...) with normal word order and if/whether for yes/no questions.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Inquire -- to ask for information politely or formally
"I would like to inquire about the contract terms." (Indagar)
Wonder -- to want to know something
"I was wondering about the closing date." (Querer saber)
Specify -- to state something exactly and clearly
"Could you specify the final price?" (Especificar)
Rephrase -- to say something again in a different way
"Could you rephrase the question, please?" (Reformular)
Confirm -- to check that something is true or correct
"I want to confirm the key terms." (Confirmar)
Assumption -- something you believe is true without proof
"Let us not make an assumption about the numbers." (Suposição)
Concern -- a worry or issue you want to ask about
"I have one concern about the timeline." (Preocupação)
Detail -- a small piece of information
"Could you give me more detail on the clause?" (Detalhe)
Relevant -- connected and important to the topic
"Please keep every question relevant to the deal." (Relevante)
Probe -- to ask deeper questions to find out more
"We need to probe the indemnity clause." (Investigar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Inquire
Wonder
Specify
Rephrase
Confirm
Assumption
Concern
Detail
Relevant
Probe

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Before the due diligence session, Simone prepares smart questions. She wants to confirm the key terms, so she will ask, "Could you tell me when the contract takes effect?" She also wants to specify the numbers: "Do you know how much the final price is?" She has one concern about the indemnity clause, so she will ask, "I was wondering why they changed it." If an answer is not clear, she will politely ask them to rephrase it, and she keeps every question relevant to the deal.

1. How does Simone ask about the price?
A "Do you know how much the final price is?"
B "How much is the price, now?"
C "Tell me the price."
2. What does she do if an answer is not clear?
A She ends the call.
B She politely asks them to rephrase it.
C She makes an assumption.
3. What is her one concern about?
A The final price.
B The meeting room.
C The indemnity clause.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Indirect Questions

GRAMMAR

Para perguntar de forma educada, use perguntas indiretas: comece com um opener (Could you tell me..., Do you know..., I was wondering..., Would you mind telling me...) e depois use a ordem NORMAL (sujeito + verbo), SEM inversao e SEM do/does/did. Para perguntas de sim/nao, use if ou whether. Direto: "Where is the office?" Indireto: "Could you tell me where the office is?" / To ask politely, use indirect questions: start with an opener and then use normal word order (subject + verb), with no inversion and no do/does/did. For yes/no questions, use if or whether.

Type / TipoStructure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Wh- questionopener + question word + subject + verb"Could you tell me where the office is?"
Yes / No questionopener + if / whether + subject + verb"Do you know if the price is final?"
Polite openersCould you tell me..., Do you know..., I was wondering..., Would you mind telling me...

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Could you tell me where the office ?"
"Do you know when the deal ?"
"I was why they changed the clause."
"Could you tell me the price is final?"
"I want to the key terms."
"Please keep every question to the deal."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?They meet on the call and Simone asks her indirect questions.
?Daniel leaves a voice note inviting Simone to the due diligence session.
?Simone checks the last details and closes the question session.
?Simone prepares a list of smart questions on her prep sheet.
?Daniel and the other side answer and confirm the key terms.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Could you tell me where the documents are?
Você poderia me dizer onde estão os documentos?
Do you know when the deal closes?
Você sabe quando o negócio fecha?
I was wondering why the clause changed.
Eu estava me perguntando por que a cláusula mudou.
Could you confirm the key terms?
Você poderia confirmar os termos principais?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best polite question for each situation.

You want to know where the documents are. How do you ask politely?
A "Where the documents?"
B "Could you tell me where the documents are?"
C "Could you tell me where are the documents?"
You did not understand the answer. What do you say?
A "Could you rephrase that, please?"
B "Repeat now."
C "I not understand."
You want to know if the price is final. How do you ask indirectly?
A "Do you know is the price final?"
B "Do you know if the price is final?"
C "You know the price final?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are preparing for a Q&A session about a deal. Record yourself asking five smart, indirect questions: confirm the key terms, specify the numbers, probe one concern, and remember to keep every question relevant and polite.

Survival Card -- Lesson 9

1Could you tell me where the documents are?Você poderia me dizer onde estão os documentos?
2Do you know when the deal closes?Você sabe quando o negócio fecha?
3I was wondering why the clause changed.Eu estava me perguntando por que a cláusula mudou.
4Could you confirm the key terms?Você poderia confirmar os termos principais?
5Would you mind rephrasing that, please?Você se importaria de reformular isso, por favor?
Aula 10 -- Pre-class

Travel English I -- Travel Vocabulary & Polite Requests

Travel vocabulary and how to make polite requests at the airport and the hotel. New vocabulary: boarding pass, gate, aisle seat, luggage, check-in, departure, customs, reservation, front desk, itinerary. Grammar: polite requests with Could I / Could you + base verb, I would like to + verb, and Would it be possible to + verb.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Boarding pass -- the pass that lets you get on the plane
"Please keep your boarding pass ready." (Passe de embarque)
Gate -- the door where you get on the plane
"The departure gate is number twenty-two." (Portão de embarque)
Aisle seat -- a seat next to the walking path on a plane
"Could I have an aisle seat, please?" (Assento no corredor)
Luggage -- the bags and cases you travel with
"I would like to check one piece of luggage." (Bagagem)
Check-in -- to register for a flight or a hotel room
"Check-in for the flight opens at seven." (Check-in / registro)
Departure -- the time when a plane or train leaves
"The departure time is nine in the morning." (Partida)
Customs -- where officers check what you bring into a country
"Go through customs after you land." (Alfândega)
Reservation -- a booking you make in advance
"I would like to confirm my reservation." (Reserva)
Front desk -- the reception desk at a hotel
"Ask at the front desk for your key." (Recepção)
Itinerary -- the plan of a trip with times and places
"Please review the itinerary for the trip." (Itinerário)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Boarding pass
Gate
Aisle seat
Luggage
Check-in
Departure
Customs
Reservation
Front desk
Itinerary

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Before her trip to Madrid, Simone reviews her itinerary. At the airport check-in she will say, "Could I have an aisle seat, please?" She has one bag, so she will ask, "Could you check my luggage to Madrid?" She keeps her boarding pass safe and finds the departure gate. After she lands, she goes through customs. At the hotel front desk she would like to confirm her reservation, so she says, "I would like to confirm my booking, and could I have a quiet room?"

1. How does Simone ask for a seat by the aisle?
A "Could I have an aisle seat, please?"
B "Could I to have an aisle seat?"
C "I want aisle seat now."
2. What does she do at the hotel front desk?
A She checks her luggage.
B She confirms her reservation.
C She finds the gate.
3. Where does she go right after she lands?
A To the departure gate.
B To the check-in desk.
C Through customs.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Polite Requests

GRAMMAR

Para pedir algo de forma educada, use Could I + verbo na forma BASE (para você mesma) ou Could you + verbo na forma BASE (para a outra pessoa), SEM to, SEM -ing e SEM -s. Use I would like + to + verbo (ou + substantivo) para um desejo educado, e Would it be possible to + verbo para ser ainda mais educada. "Could I have a seat?" (não "Could I to have a seat?"). / To ask for something politely, use Could I or Could you + the base verb, with no to, no -ing and no -s. Use I would like + to + verb for a polite wish, and Would it be possible to + verb to be extra polite.

Type / TipoStructure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Ask for yourselfCould I + base verb"Could I have an aisle seat?"
Ask the other personCould you + base verb"Could you check my luggage?"
Polite wishI would like + to + verb / + noun"I would like to confirm my reservation."
Extra politeWould it be possible to + verb"Would it be possible to get a late check-out?"

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Could I an aisle seat, please?"
"Could you my luggage to Madrid?"
"I would like confirm my reservation."
"Please keep your boarding ready."
"Go through after you land."
"The departure is number twenty-two."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?At the check-in desk, Simone asks for an aisle seat and checks her luggage.
?Daniel leaves a voice note with the itinerary for the Madrid trip.
?At the front desk, Simone confirms her reservation and asks for a quiet room.
?Simone packs her bag and gets to the airport early.
?She goes through customs and takes a taxi to the hotel.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Could I have an aisle seat, please?
Você poderia me dar um assento no corredor, por favor?
Could you check my luggage to Madrid?
Você poderia despachar minha bagagem para Madri?
I would like to confirm my reservation.
Eu gostaria de confirmar minha reserva.
Could you tell me where the departure gate is?
Você poderia me dizer onde fica o portão de embarque?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best polite request for each situation.

You are at check-in and want a seat by the aisle. How do you ask politely?
A "Could I to have an aisle seat?"
B "Could I have an aisle seat, please?"
C "I want aisle seat."
You want the agent to check your bag. What do you say?
A "Could you check my luggage, please?"
B "Could you checking my luggage?"
C "Check my bag now."
You arrive at the hotel and want to confirm your booking. How do you ask?
A "I would like confirm my reservation."
B "I would like to confirm my reservation."
C "I confirm reservation, please."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are traveling to Madrid for a contract signing. Record yourself making five polite requests: ask for an aisle seat, ask the agent to check your luggage, ask where the gate is, confirm your hotel reservation, and ask for a late check-out.

Survival Card -- Lesson 10

1Could I have an aisle seat, please?Você poderia me dar um assento no corredor, por favor?
2Could you check my luggage to Madrid?Você poderia despachar minha bagagem para Madri?
3I would like to confirm my reservation.Eu gostaria de confirmar minha reserva.
4Could you tell me where the departure gate is?Você poderia me dizer onde fica o portão de embarque?
5Would it be possible to get a late check-out?Seria possível fazer um check-out mais tarde?
Aula 11 -- Pre-class

Contract Vocabulary -- Legal Nouns & Defining Clauses

Key legal nouns for reading a contract and how to point to one specific part with defining clauses. New vocabulary: clause, party, obligation, liability, breach, term, warranty, indemnity. Grammar: defining clauses with that / which (for things) and that / who (for people and parties).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Clause -- a separate part or rule in a contract
"This is the clause that limits liability." (Cláusula)
Party -- one of the sides that signs a contract
"Each party must sign the contract." (Parte)
Obligation -- something a party must do by law
"Payment on time is a key obligation." (Obrigação)
Liability -- legal responsibility for damage or loss
"This clause limits our liability." (Responsabilidade civil)
Breach -- when a party breaks a rule of the contract
"A breach of this clause is serious." (Violação / quebra de contrato)
Term -- a single condition or defined word in a contract
"This term defines what a delay is." (Termo / condição)
Warranty -- a promise that something will work or be true
"The warranty which covers the software lasts one year." (Garantia)
Indemnity -- a promise to pay for loss or damage you cause
"The indemnity clause protects the other party." (Indenização)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Clause
Party
Obligation
Liability
Breach
Term
Warranty
Indemnity

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Before the review, Simone reads the new contract with the German partner. There is a clause that limits the liability of each party, but the limit is too high for her company. The section that lists the obligations is clear, but the deadline it sets is very tight. The warranty which covers the software lasts only six months, and she wants a longer period. Finally, there is a term that defines a breach, and Simone wants to make it precise, because a breach that is not well defined creates risk.

1. What does the first clause do?
A It limits the liability of each party.
B It lists the obligations of the parties.
C It defines the warranty.
2. What is Simone's problem with the warranty?
A It covers the wrong product.
B It lasts only six months, and she wants a longer period.
C It is missing from the contract.
3. Why does she want a precise definition of a breach?
A Because a breach is never serious.
B Because a breach that is not well defined creates risk.
C Because the deadline is too long.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Defining Clauses

GRAMMAR

Para dizer DE QUAL parte do contrato você está falando, use uma defining clause. Para COISAS (cláusula, garantia, termo) use that ou which; para PESSOAS ou PARTES use that ou who. O verbo vem logo depois: "the clause that LIMITS liability". Nunca use what e não coloque vírgula antes de that/which numa defining clause. / To say WHICH part of the contract you mean, use a defining clause. For THINGS (clause, warranty, term) use that or which; for PEOPLE or PARTIES use that or who. The verb comes right after: "the clause that limits...". Never use what, and use no comma before that/which in a defining clause.

We talk about / Falamos deWord / PalavraExample / Exemplo
A thing / Uma coisathat / which"the clause that limits liability"
A thing / Uma coisawhich"the warranty which covers the software"
A party / Uma partethat / who"the party that signs first"
A negative idea / Ideia negativathat + is not"a breach that is not defined"

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"This is the clause limits our liability."
"It is the warranty covers the software."
"The party signs first keeps a copy."
"A of this clause is serious."
"Each must sign the contract."
"Payment on time is a key ."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?She notes the clause that limits the liability and the section that lists the obligations.
?Daniel sends the new contract with the German partner and asks Simone to read it.
?At the review meeting, Simone raises each point with the other party.
?Simone reads the document and marks every clause that is not clear.
?She prepares her questions about the warranty and the definition of a breach.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

This is the clause that limits our liability.
Esta é a cláusula que limita nossa responsabilidade.
It is the warranty which covers the software.
É a garantia que cobre o software.
Each party must meet its obligations.
Cada parte deve cumprir suas obrigações.
A breach that is not defined creates risk.
Uma violação que não está definida cria risco.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the correct sentence for each situation.

You want to point to the specific clause about liability. What do you say?
A "This is the clause what limits liability."
B "This is the clause that limits liability."
C "This is the clause who limits liability."
You describe the side that signs the contract first. How do you say it?
A "The party that signs first keeps a copy."
B "The party which signs first keeps a copy."
C "The party what signs first keeps a copy."
You want a longer promise for the software. What do you ask about?
A The breach which covers the software.
B The warranty which covers the software.
C The party which covers the software.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are reviewing a contract at work. Record yourself describing five parts of it, using a defining clause for each: the clause that limits liability, the section that lists the obligations, the warranty which covers the product, the party that signs first, and the term that defines a breach.

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1This is the clause that limits our liability.Esta é a cláusula que limita nossa responsabilidade.
2Each party must meet its obligations.Cada parte deve cumprir suas obrigações.
3It is the warranty which covers the software.É a garantia que cobre o software.
4A breach that is not defined creates risk.Uma violação que não está definida cria risco.
5Could you send a draft that includes the indemnity?Você poderia enviar uma minuta que inclua a indenização?
Aula 12 -- Pre-class

Reading Contracts II -- Conditional Clauses with if / unless / provided

Read the conditions of a contract and say when something happens, on what condition, and what would stop it. New vocabulary: provision, governing law, jurisdiction, terminate, comply, waiver, amend, enforce. Grammar: conditional clauses with if (the condition), unless (= if not), and provided (= only if this is true).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Provision -- a specific rule or condition in a contract
"There is a provision that ends the contract." (Disposição / cláusula específica)
Governing law -- the law that controls the contract
"The governing law is German law." (Lei aplicável)
Jurisdiction -- the country or court that decides a dispute
"We prefer Brazilian jurisdiction." (Jurisdição / foro)
Terminate -- to end a contract
"The contract terminates if a party does not pay." (Rescindir / encerrar)
Comply -- to follow a rule of the contract
"Each party must comply with the deadline." (Cumprir)
Waiver -- when a party gives up a right on purpose
"We accept the waiver only in writing." (Renúncia / dispensa)
Amend -- to change part of a contract officially
"We can amend the contract in writing." (Alterar / emendar)
Enforce -- to make a rule of the contract happen by law
"We enforce the contract from June." (Fazer valer / executar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Provision
Governing law
Jurisdiction
Terminate
Comply
Waiver
Amend
Enforce

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Simone reads the conditions of the new draft. There is a provision that says the contract terminates if a party does not pay within thirty days. She thinks this is too strict, so she will ask for sixty days, provided the partner accepts a small interest. The draft also sets German governing law, but Simone prefers Brazilian jurisdiction, unless the partner has a strong reason. Finally, she will not accept the waiver unless it is signed by both parties. If the partner agrees, the parties will comply with the new terms and enforce the contract from June.

1. When does the contract terminate?
A If a party does not pay within thirty days.
B If the partner changes the governing law.
C If both parties sign the waiver.
2. On what condition will Simone ask for sixty days?
A Provided the contract follows German law.
B Provided the partner accepts a small interest.
C Provided the waiver is removed.
3. When will Simone accept the waiver?
A Unless the partner pays on time.
B Only if it is signed by both parties.
C Only if the governing law is Brazilian.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Conditional Clauses

GRAMMAR

Para dizer EM QUE CONDIÇÃO algo acontece num contrato, use uma conditional clause. If = se (a condição). Unless = a não ser que / se não (= if not). Provided = desde que (= só se isto for verdade). O verbo fica no presente nas duas partes: "the contract ends IF a party FAILS to pay". Não use will depois de if/unless/provided. / To say ON WHAT CONDITION something happens in a contract, use a conditional clause. If = the condition. Unless = if not. Provided = only if this is true. Use the present in both parts: "the contract ends if a party fails to pay". Do not use will after if, unless or provided.

Word / PalavraMeaning / SentidoExample / Exemplo
if / sethe condition"The contract terminates if a party does not pay."
unless / a não ser que= if not"We wait, unless the partner agrees."
provided / desde queonly if this is true"We accept it provided it is in writing."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The contract terminates a party does not pay."
"We wait, the partner agrees."
"We accept the waiver it is in writing."
"There is a that ends the contract."
"Each party must with the deadline."
"We can the contract in writing."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?She decides to ask for sixty days, provided the partner accepts a small interest.
?Daniel sends the new draft and asks Simone to read the conditions.
?At the review meeting, Simone proposes the new conditions to the other party.
?Simone reads the provision that ends the contract if a party does not pay in thirty days.
?She marks the governing law, because she prefers Brazilian courts unless the partner has a strong reason.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

The contract terminates if a party does not pay.
O contrato é rescindido se uma parte não pagar.
We accept the waiver provided it is in writing.
Aceitamos a renúncia desde que seja por escrito.
We prefer Brazilian jurisdiction unless the partner disagrees.
Preferimos a jurisdição brasileira a não ser que o parceiro discorde.
If the partner agrees, we enforce the contract from June.
Se o parceiro concordar, fazemos valer o contrato a partir de junho.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the correct sentence for each situation.

You want to say the contract ends only when a party fails to pay. What do you say?
A "The contract terminates if a party will not pay."
B "The contract terminates if a party does not pay."
C "The contract terminates unless a party does not pay."
You will accept a change only when it is in writing. How do you say it?
A "We amend the contract provided it is in writing."
B "We amend the contract unless it is in writing."
C "We amend the contract if it is not in writing."
You say that you will wait if the partner does not agree. How do you say it?
A "We sign unless the partner agrees."
B "We wait unless the partner agrees."
C "We wait provided the partner agrees."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are reviewing the conditions of a contract at work. Record yourself describing four conditions, using if, unless and provided: when the contract terminates, the governing law you prefer, when you accept a waiver, and when each party must comply with a deadline.

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1The contract terminates if a party does not pay.O contrato é rescindido se uma parte não pagar.
2We accept the waiver provided it is in writing.Aceitamos a renúncia desde que seja por escrito.
3We prefer Brazilian jurisdiction unless the partner disagrees.Preferimos a jurisdição brasileira a não ser que o parceiro discorde.
4Each party must comply with the deadline.Cada parte deve cumprir o prazo.
5If the partner agrees, we enforce the contract from June.Se o parceiro concordar, fazemos valer o contrato a partir de junho.
Aula 13 -- Pre-class

Contract Vocabulary III -- Obligations & Permissions with must / shall / may

Read the duties of a contract and say what each party must do, shall do, and may do. New vocabulary: undertake, deliver, notify, grant, prohibit, oblige, perform, refrain. Grammar: modal verbs must (obligation), shall (a formal legal duty), may (permission), and must not (prohibition).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Undertake -- to promise to do something in a contract
"The seller undertakes to keep the data safe." (Comprometer-se / obrigar-se)
Deliver -- to hand over the goods or service on time
"The seller shall deliver the software by August." (Entregar)
Notify -- to tell the other party officially, in writing
"Each party must notify the other before a change." (Notificar / avisar)
Grant -- to give a right or permission to the other party
"The contract grants the buyer a license." (Conceder / outorgar)
Prohibit -- to say that something is not allowed
"The contract prohibits sharing the code." (Proibir)
Oblige -- to force a party to do a duty by the contract
"The clause obliges both sides to act in good faith." (Obrigar)
Perform -- to do what the contract asks you to do
"Each party must perform its duties on time." (Cumprir / executar)
Refrain -- to choose not to do something, on purpose
"Both sides must refrain from hiring each other's staff." (Abster-se)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Undertake
Deliver
Notify
Grant
Prohibit
Oblige
Perform
Refrain

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Simone reads the duties section of the new draft. The seller shall deliver the software by August, and each party must notify the other in writing before any change. The contract grants the buyer a license, so the buyer may use the software for internal projects, but must not share the code with a third party. The seller undertakes to perform every duty in good faith, and both sides must refrain from hiring each other's staff for one year. Simone thinks the indemnity duty is too heavy, so she will propose that each party only indemnifies a delay it causes.

1. What must the seller do, and by when?
A The seller shall deliver the software by August.
