12
Reading Contracts II
Conditional Clauses with if / unless / provided -- 27 slides
13
Contract Vocabulary III
Obligations & Permissions with must / shall / may -- 27 slides
14
Resolving Disputes
Arbitration vocabulary and the first conditional in disputes -- 27 slides
15
Reading Contracts IV
Drafting vocabulary and the passive voice in clauses -- 27 slides
16
Closing the Deal
Signing, completion and reported speech -- 27 slides
17
Leading a Meeting
Chairing, managing the floor and verb patterns -- 27 slides
18
Negotiating Terms
Bargaining language, making offers and the second conditional -- 27 slides
19
Handling Objections
Acknowledging concerns and concession connectors: however, nevertheless -- 27 slides
20
Social English
Conversation at the corporate dinner: social vocabulary and the present perfect for experience -- 27 slides

Supplementary Materials -- Lesson 1

Lesson 1 — Who Is Simone?

Podcast
Business English Pod — Socializing: Starting a Conversation in English (BEP 44c)

A free episode on introducing yourself and breaking the ice at work events: giving your name and role, opening with easy questions and keeping small talk going. Set at an HR conference with three colleagues meeting for the first time.

Listen once, then write your own 3-sentence self-introduction for a meeting.

Listen on Spotify ↗

TED Talk
Your body language may shape who you are -- Amy Cuddy

Powerful talk about confidence and body language.

Tip: Watch with English subtitles.

Watch on TED ↗

Podcast
The English We Speak — The works

A short BBC Learning English episode explaining the everyday expression 'the works', meaning 'everything' or 'with all the extras', with a quick, clear example conversation.

Listen twice, then use 'the works' in one sentence about your own life.

Listen on Spotify ↗

Lesson 2 — My Daily Routine

Podcast
Real English Conversations — English Job Interviews with Lindsay McMahon & All Ears English (Pt. 2)

A free episode featuring All Ears English host Lindsay McMahon on how to be the dream candidate in an English job interview: how to talk about your strengths, handle tricky questions and ask smart questions. Great input for describing your work.

Listen twice: first for the overall advice, then to note phrases you can reuse to describe your job.

Listen on Spotify ↗

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 1 (Netflix)

Corporate law drama with authentic legal vocabulary. Watch the first 30 minutes.

Tip: Watch with English subtitles. Note how lawyers describe their work.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
6 Minute English (BBC) — Flexible working

Neil and Georgina discuss whether working from home or flexible hours beats the traditional 9-to-5 office job. Practical vocabulary about modern work routines.

Listen during your commute and write down 3 new phrases about work.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 — Email Essentials

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 2: "Errors and Omissions"

A legal drama where lawyers live in their inboxes. Watch how characters open emails, mark a clear subject, attach documents, and forward them to the right people -- the exact email code from today.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 opening or closing lines you hear or read on screen, then reuse one in an email of your own.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
Espresso English — 30 Essential Business English Phrases for Letters & E-Mails

A free Espresso English episode packed with 30 ready-to-use phrases for professional letters and emails: openings, polite requests and closings you can drop straight into your writing.

Copy 5 phrases into a template and reuse them in your next email.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "How to write a formal email"

A clear lesson on professional email structure: subject line, greeting, body, and sign off, plus the difference between formal and informal register. Directly useful for your emails to international counsel.

After watching, write a 5-line email to a fictional counterpart: open, make one request, mention an attachment, and sign off. Then read it out loud.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — Phone & Video Calls

Series
Industry (HBO) -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Induction"

A fast-paced corporate drama set in finance, full of phone and conference calls. Notice how characters open a call, identify themselves, deal with bad lines, and close with a clear next step -- exactly the phone code from today.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 phrases people use to open or close a call. Then record yourself reusing one of them with your own context.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Adjusting to Remote Work During the Coronavirus Crisis

HBS's Tsedal Neeley on leading remote teams and running effective virtual meetings: launch clearly, set communication norms, and keep remote colleagues engaged and heard.

List the phrases for opening a call, handling a weak connection, and asking someone to speak up.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Is talking on the phone embarrassing? 6 Minute English

A short, practical lesson on professional phone language: introducing yourself, asking someone to hold on, dealing with a bad connection, leaving a voicemail, and ending the call politely. Directly useful for your calls with international counsel.

After watching, record a 60-second voicemail in English about a contract point. Use at least four new words and the three phases: open, clarify, close.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 5 — Small Talk That Works

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

A legal drama full of networking and small talk. Watch how the characters break the ice, ask open questions, find common ground, and keep in touch -- exactly the conversation moves from today's lesson.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 questions characters use to start or build a conversation. Then record yourself reusing one with your own context.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Improve Your Impromptu Speaking

Stanford's Matt Abrahams on speaking off the cuff: managing nerves and using simple structures like "what, so what, now what" that make small talk and impromptu conversation easier.

Try his "what, so what, now what" structure out loud on one everyday topic.

Open episode ↗

YouTube / TED
TED -- "The art of asking questions" / BBC Learning English: "Making small talk"

A short, practical lesson on conversation: introducing yourself, asking open questions, finding common ground, and ending a chat while keeping in touch. Directly useful for your networking at legal events.

After watching, note three phrases the speaker uses to connect with the audience, then record a 60-second self-introduction using at least four new expressions from today.

Watch on TED ↗

Lesson 6 — Meeting Basics

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 3: "Inside Track"

A legal drama with sharp meeting scenes. Watch how the lawyers chair a discussion, raise points, recap decisions, and assign action items -- and notice the polite requests with could and the advice with should.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 sentences with can, could, or should from a meeting scene. Then record yourself reusing one in your own meeting context.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Why Meetings Go Wrong (And How to Fix Them)

Meeting-science researcher Steven Rogelberg explains why most managers are never trained to run meetings and shares fixes: tight agendas, welcoming attendees, and ending on time. Practical, hands-on guidance for chairing a discussion well.

Note the concrete verbs he uses for agenda-setting and wrap-up, then reuse them in your next meeting.

Open episode ↗

YouTube / BBC
Using 'can', 'could', 'be able to' and 'manage to' - 6 Minute Grammar

A short, practical lesson on taking part in meetings in English: opening with the agenda, raising a point, asking for a recap, and making polite requests with can, could, and should. Directly useful for your project meetings at Telefonica.

After watching, note three polite phrases with could or should, then record a 60-second meeting opening using at least four new words from today.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 7 — Expressing Opinions

Series
The Good Wife -- Season 1, Episode 5: "Crash"

A legal drama full of courtroom and office debates. Watch how the lawyers state their point of view, build an argument, persuade others, and look for common ground -- and notice the polite ways they disagree.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 sentences with I think, in my view, or I disagree from a debate scene. Then record yourself reusing one in your own work context.

Disponível no streaming: Paramount+

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — A Debate Champion on How to Have More Productive Disagreements at Work

Two-time world debate champion Bo Seo shows how to disagree in healthier, more effective ways — building a strong argument, listening to persuade, and finding common ground rather than winning. Directly on point for stating opinions at work.

Listen for how he frames a counter-argument politely; copy those opening phrases.

Open episode ↗

YouTube / BBC
Expressions for agreeing and disagreeing in English | Learn English with Cambridge

A short, practical lesson on expressing opinions in English: opening with I think and in my view, supporting them with an argument, and agreeing or disagreeing politely. Directly useful for your discussions and negotiations at Telefonica.

After watching, note three opinion phrases, then record a 60-second opinion on a work topic using at least four new words from today.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 8 — Giving Updates

Series
Industry -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Induction"

A drama set in a global investment bank, full of project updates and status meetings. Watch how the analysts report progress, name milestones, and explain what is on track or still pending -- and notice the present continuous when they say what is happening right now.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 sentences in the present continuous about what someone is doing. Then record yourself giving a one-minute update on your own project.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — The Conversations You Should Be Having with Your Manager

Executive coach Melody Wilding explains how to keep your manager informed: getting visibility for your work, flagging problems early, aligning on priorities, and reporting progress clearly. Practical language for updating the people you report to.

Focus on the phrases she suggests for surfacing a delay or blocker without sounding defensive.

Open episode ↗

YouTube / BBC
Learn English Tenses: The Present Continuous

A short, practical lesson on the present continuous: am/is/are + verb-ing for actions happening now, with time expressions like right now and at the moment. Directly useful for the project updates you give at Telefonica.

After watching, note three time expressions, then record a 60-second project update using at least four new words from today.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 9 — Asking Smart Questions

Series
The Good Wife -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

A legal drama full of smart questioning. Watch how the lawyers ask careful, polite questions to get information from clients and witnesses -- listen for indirect questions like "Could you tell me..." and "Do you know whether...".

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 indirect questions you hear. Then turn each one into a direct question and notice the change in word order.

Disponível no streaming: Paramount+

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Ask Better Questions

Harvard professors Leslie John and Alison Wood Brooks (authors of the HBR article 'The Surprising Power of Questions') discuss how to ask more and better questions at work — probing deeper, following up, and building rapport.

Write down two follow-up question templates from the episode and try them in your next conversation.

Open episode ↗

YouTube / BBC
Indirect Questions in English: How to Ask for Things Politely

A short, practical lesson on indirect questions: how openers like "Could you tell me" and "Do you know" change the word order and make a question sound polite. Directly useful for the Q&A sessions you run at Telefonica.

After watching, write five indirect questions about a real deal, then record yourself asking them using at least four new words from today.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 10 — Travel English I

Series
Up in the Air (2009) -- film

A business traveler who lives in airports and hotels. Watch the check-in, security, boarding and hotel scenes, and listen for the polite requests and travel words you learned: boarding pass, gate, aisle seat, check-in, front desk.

Watch the first 30 minutes with English subtitles. Write down 5 travel words you hear and 3 polite requests with Could I or Could you.

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Podcast
Easy English Podcast — Travelling in Airports: Vocabulary, Useful Phrases, Real-life Travel Scenarios

A practical episode for travelers covering airport English from check-in to customs — booking, luggage, missing bags and polite complaints — with real dialogues and useful phrases.

Practise the check-in phrases out loud before your next trip; repeat each one after the speaker.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube / BBC
How to be polite in English - a Mini English Lesson

A short, practical lesson on making polite requests when you travel: how Could I and Could you make a request sound polite, and the base verb form that comes after them. Directly useful for the business trips you take for closings and signings.

After watching, write five polite requests for a real trip, then record yourself asking them using at least four travel words from today.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 11 — Contract Vocabulary

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 1 "Pilot"

A legal drama set in a corporate law firm. The lawyers constantly discuss clauses, liability, breach and the obligations of each party. Listen for how they describe one specific part of a contract with defining clauses ("the clause that...", "the term that...").

Watch the first 30 minutes with English subtitles. Write down 5 legal nouns you hear and 3 sentences with that or which.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
The Study Legal English Podcast — E96.1: Sarah Fox — How to Write a 500-Word Contract and Know It's Good

O Study Legal English entrevista Sarah Fox sobre escrever um contrato claro de 500 palavras: as clausulas essenciais que todo contrato comercial precisa e um checklist para revisa-lo.

Liste as clausulas-chave que ela menciona e leia cada uma em voz alta.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube / BBC
How to use who, that, and which in Defining Relative Clauses

A short, clear lesson on defining relative clauses: when to use that or which for things, and that or who for people. Directly useful for describing the clauses and parties in the contracts you review.

After watching, write five sentences about a contract using a defining clause in each, then record yourself reading them with at least four legal words from today.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — Reading Contracts II

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 2 "Errors and Omissions"

The lawyers negotiate the conditions of a deal: when an agreement terminates, what each party must comply with, and on what condition they will sign. Listen for sentences with if, unless and provided, and for words like provision, jurisdiction and waiver.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 conditions you hear (one with if, one with unless, one with provided) and the legal nouns around them.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
The Study Legal English Podcast — E98: Mark Anderson — IP Contracts and IP Terminology

O Study Legal English conversa com o advogado de PI Mark Anderson sobre contratos de propriedade intelectual e a terminologia usada para definir, licenciar e fazer valer seus termos.

Anote cinco termos contratuais do episodio e defina cada um em uma linha.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube / BBC
The first conditional - 6 Minute Grammar

A short, clear lesson on how to talk about real conditions with if, unless and provided that, and why we use the present tense after them. Directly useful for reading and discussing the conditions in the contracts you review.

After watching, write five conditions about a contract (using if, unless and provided), then record yourself reading them with at least four legal words from today.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 13 — Contract Vocabulary III

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 3 "Inside Track"

The lawyers argue about the duties of each party in a deal: what one side shall deliver, what each party must notify, what the buyer may do, and what is prohibited. Listen for sentences with must, shall and may, and for words like undertake, deliver, grant and refrain.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 duties you hear (one with must, one with shall, one with may) and the legal verbs around them.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
The Study Legal English Podcast — E99: Ken Adams — Cracking the Contract Drafting Code

O Study Legal English com Ken Adams sobre como os contratos declaram os deveres de cada parte: por que os advogados usam 'shall', 'must' e 'may' e como evitar linguagem de obrigacao ambigua.

Grave tres clausulas: uma com 'shall', uma com 'must' e uma com 'may'.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube / BBC
Modal verbs explained - a Mini English Lesson

A short, clear lesson on how to talk about obligation, permission and prohibition with must, must not and may, and why we use the base verb after them. Directly useful for reading and discussing the duties in the contracts you review.

After watching, write five duties about a contract (using must, shall, may and must not), then record yourself reading them with at least four legal words from today.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — Resolving Disputes

Series
Suits -- Season 2, Episode 6 "All In"

The lawyers face a dispute and weigh a settlement against going to a hearing. Listen for how they talk about a claim, a settlement and a binding decision, and notice the first-conditional sentences: "if we settle now, we will...", "if they refuse, we will...".

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 first-conditional sentences you hear (if + present, will + verb) and the dispute words around them.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
The Study Legal English Podcast — E58: An Introduction to Arbitration

Monologo do Study Legal English que introduz a arbitragem como forma de resolucao de disputas: como as partes a escolhem em vez do tribunal, como funciona a audiencia e por que a sentenca arbitral e vinculante.

Anote, com suas palavras, a diferenca entre mediacao e arbitragem.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube / BBC
The First Conditional: The Grammar Gameshow Episode 10

A short, clear lesson on how to talk about real future results with the first conditional, and why the present goes after if. Directly useful for explaining the steps of a dispute clause in the contracts you review.

After watching, write five first-conditional sentences about a dispute, then record yourself reading them with at least four dispute words from today.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 — Reading Contracts IV

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 9 "Undefeated"

The lawyers draft and review a deal, reading clauses line by line before the contract is executed. Listen for how they talk about a draft, an annex and the definitions, and notice the passive voice: "the agreement is signed", "the terms are stipulated".

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 passive sentences you hear (be + past participle) and the drafting words around them.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
The Study Legal English Podcast — E133: Ken Adams — A Manual of Style for Contract Drafting (5th edn.)

O Study Legal English com Ken Adams sobre redigir e interpretar contratos: como as definicoes sao construidas e como escrever clausulas mais claras e menos ambiguas.

Escolha uma dica de redacao mais clara e reescreva uma clausula prolixa usando-a.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube / BBC
Present and past passives - 6 Minute Grammar

A short, clear lesson on how to form the passive voice and when to use it, including how to add "by" to say who does the action. Directly useful for reading and explaining the clauses of the contracts you review.

After watching, write five passive sentences about a contract, then record yourself reading them with at least four drafting words from today.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 16 — Closing the Deal

Series
Suits -- Season 2, Episode 6 "All In"

A deal goes to the closing: the parties finalize the price, sign and countersign the documents, and the proceeds are transferred. Listen for how the lawyers report what the other side said, and notice reported speech: "he said the price was final", "she told me she would attend".

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 reported sentences you hear (said / told me + the verb one step back) and the closing words around them.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
The Study Legal English Podcast — E137: Barrie J. Roberts — Negotiating Success: Principled Negotiation for English Learners

O Study Legal English com Barrie J. Roberts sobre negociacao baseada em principios para quem aprende ingles: chegar a um acordo, confirmar os termos e relatar o que cada lado disse.

Grave duas linhas que confirmam o que foi combinado ('As we agreed...').

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube / BBC
Reported Speech Using SAY and TELL — English Grammar

A short, clear lesson on how to report what someone said: the difference between said and told, and how the verb moves one step back (is → was, will → would). Directly useful for briefing your team after a closing call.

After watching, write five reported sentences about a deal, then record yourself reading them with at least four closing words from today.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 17 — Leading a Meeting

Series
Industry -- Season 1, Episode 4 "Sesh"

Watch how a senior banker chairs a tense team meeting: stating the objective, allocating time, inviting each attendee to speak and proposing a vote. Listen for the verb patterns a chair uses: "I suggest moving on", "I propose to vote", "let's avoid going over time".

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 sentences the chair uses to manage the meeting, and mark whether each verb is followed by -ing or by to.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
Business English Pod — Meeting in English: Starting a Meeting (BEP 42c)

A free episode on how to open and lead a meeting: getting everyone's attention, stating the purpose and goals, and reviewing the agenda before you begin. Set at a shipping company as a manager chairs a meeting about a budget shortfall.

Note the phrases for opening a meeting and try running your next standup with them.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube / BBC
Gerund or infinitive? Ask BBC Learning English

A short, clear lesson on the verb patterns from today: which verbs take the -ing form (suggest, recommend, avoid) and which take to + verb (propose, decide, agree). Directly useful for leading a meeting with confidence.

After watching, write five chairing sentences (open, allocate, suggest, propose, adjourn), then record yourself reading them with at least four new words from today.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 18 — Negotiating Terms

Series
Suits -- Season 2, Episode 1 "She Knows"

Watch how the lawyers bargain over the terms of a deal: making counteroffers, trading concessions and using leverage to avoid a deadlock. Listen for second conditional sentences they use to picture possible outcomes: "If we offered X, they would accept Y".

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 sentences where a character uses "if + past, would" to make an offer, and underline the trade-off in each one.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
The Business English Podcast — Defending Positions in Tough Negotiations (BEP 159)

A free negotiation episode on making and responding to offers: how to handle a lowball offer, hold your position, use power phrases, and avoid language that accidentally signals agreement.

Listen once, then practice countering a lowball offer out loud in one sentence.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube / BBC
The second conditional - 6 Minute Grammar

A short, clear lesson on the grammar from today: if + past simple, would + verb, to talk about imaginary or unlikely situations. Directly useful for picturing possible deals at the negotiation table.

After watching, write five second conditional sentences for a negotiation (price, contract, payment, delivery, deadline), then record yourself reading them with at least four new words from today.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 19 — Handling Objections

Series
Suits -- Season 2, Episode 6 "All In"

Watch how the lawyers handle objections from clients and opponents: they acknowledge the concern, stay calm and answer with a strong contrast. Listen for how they reassure a reluctant client and overcome the objection instead of arguing.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 moments where a character acknowledges a concern before answering, and underline the connector (however / nevertheless / on the other hand) in each one.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
The Business English Podcast — Handling Client Objections Like a Pro (BEP 152)

A free episode with a three-step formula for handling objections: mirror the objection, suggest a hypothetical solution, and adjust your proposal to turn resistance into a deal.

Write down the 3 steps and rehearse them with a real objection you often hear.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube / BBC
BBC Learning English -- "Linkers of contrast: however, nevertheless, on the other hand"

A short, clear lesson on the grammar from today: how to use concession connectors to join two contrasting ideas formally, and where the comma goes. Directly useful for answering objections professionally.

After watching, write five sentences where you acknowledge a concern and then answer it with a connector. Record yourself reading them with at least four new words from today.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 20 — Social English

Series
Suits -- Season 3, Episode 4 "Conflict of Interest"

Watch the social side of business: lawyers mingle at events and dinners, give compliments, make light small talk and toast to a deal. Listen for how they keep the conversation friendly before any business is discussed.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 social phrases a character uses to compliment someone or open a conversation, and underline any present perfect (have/has + participle).

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
All Ears English — Why Relationships Determine Your Career Success

A practical All Ears English episode on the social side of professional life: how building relationships and connection (not just transactions) drives your career, with the warm, natural phrasing you need at business events and dinners.

Note the connection phrases the hosts model and try one at your next networking chat.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube / BBC
BBC Learning English -- "The Present Perfect: Have you ever...?"

A short, clear lesson on today's grammar: how to use the present perfect (have/has + past participle) to talk about experience, and how to ask Have you ever...? Directly useful for small talk at a dinner.

After watching, write five present perfect sentences about your own experience, then record yourself making conversation with at least four new words from today.

Watch on YouTube ↗

The Dream Packing Words The Code Getting There Practice Your Turn Wrap-Up
The Dream

Simone Marques -- Self Introduction

From "Forever Intermediate" to conducting meetings and negotiations in English.

The Dream

Let's Talk About You

"What is the most interesting part of your work at Telefonica?"
"Do you use English at work? In what situations?"
"What would you like to say in English that you cannot say today?"
Today's Promise

By the End of Today, You Will...

1 Introduce yourself professionally in English, including your role, responsibilities, and experience at Telefonica.
2 Use 10 essential corporate and legal vocabulary words with confidence.
3 Have a professional conversation with a colleague from another office.
Packing Words

Words 1-4

A formal written legal document between two or more parties
Contract
A formal written legal document between two or more parties
"Simone works on international contracts at Telefonica."
To discuss terms in order to reach an agreement
Negotiate
To discuss terms in order to reach an agreement
"We need to negotiate the terms of this agreement."
An arrangement accepted by all parties involved
Agreement
An arrangement accepted by all parties involved
"Both parties signed the agreement last week."
Related to a large company or business group
Corporate
Related to a large company or business group
"She handles corporate legal matters for the company."
0 / 4 words revealed
Packing Words

Words 5-8

Having the duty to manage or take care of something
Responsible
Having the duty to manage or take care of something
"I am responsible for reviewing all contracts."
At the present time; right now
Currently
At the present time; right now
"We are currently working on a new international deal."
To handle, manage, or be involved with something
Deal
To handle, manage, or be involved with something
"I deal with international clients every day."
A gathering of people for discussion or decisions
Meeting
A gathering of people for discussion or decisions
"The meeting with the London office is at three."
0 / 4 words revealed
Packing Words

Words 9-10

A person you work with in a professional setting
Colleague
A person you work with in a professional setting
"My colleague from Germany will join the call."
To present yourself or someone else formally
Introduce
To present yourself or someone else formally
"Let me introduce myself. I am Simone Marques."
0 / 2 words revealed
Review

Say It All

Click each word to listen. Repeat each one twice.

Contract
Negotiate
Agreement
Corporate
Responsible
Currently
Deal
Meeting
Colleague
Introduce

Words -- Packed.

Now let us see the grammar behind them.

The Code

Grammar Discovery

Look at the highlighted words. What do they have in common?

Simone works at Telefonica. She reviews contracts every day.
She does not work in the marketing department.
Does she negotiate contracts?
They deal with international partners every day.
FormStructureExample
AffirmativeSubject + verb (base/+s)She works at Telefonica.
NegativeSubject + do/does not + verbShe does not work on weekends.
QuestionDo/Does + subject + verb?Does she negotiate contracts?
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"She work at Telefonica."
"She works at Telefonica."
"Does she works with partners?"
"Does she work with partners?"
Practice

Fill in: Present Simple

"She ___works at Telefonica every day."
"He ___does not work in the legal department."
"___Does she negotiate contracts with partners?"
"They ___deal with international agreements every week."
Comprehension Check

Do You Understand?

1. In "She works at Telefonica," why do we add -s to "work"?
Because the subject is "she" (third person singular). We add -s or -es to the verb.
2. Why is "Does she works?" incorrect?
After "does," the verb stays in the base form (no -s). "Does" already carries the third-person marker.
3. When do we use Present Simple?
For facts, habits, routines, and things that are generally true. Example: "I deal with contracts every day."

Grammar -- Cracked.

Time to see the language in action.

Getting There

Your Business Card

SM
Simone Quiles de Santana Marques
Corporate Lawyer -- Contracts & M&A
Company: Telefônica
Department: Legal
Offices: Brazil, UK, Germany
Focus: International Agreements
Comprehension

About the Card

1. What is Simone's role at Telefonica?
She is a corporate lawyer, responsible for contracts and M&A.
2. Which offices does she work with?
Brazil, the United Kingdom, and Germany.
3. What is her main focus?
International agreements.
Listening

Professional Introduction

Listen to a lawyer introducing herself at an international meeting.

0:000:00
Speed
1. What is the speaker's name and role?
Simone Marques, corporate lawyer at Telefonica
2. What is she responsible for?
Contracts and mergers and acquisitions
3. Which countries does she work with?
Brazil, the United Kingdom, and Germany
Comprehension

About the Introduction

1. How many years has Simone worked in corporate law?
Over twenty years.
2. What does she negotiate?
Terms with partners from the UK and Germany.
3. What does she review every day?
Legal documents.
Dialogue

Meeting James Wilson

J
Good morning! I do not think we have met. I am James Wilson from the London office.
S
Nice to meet you, James. I am Simone Marques. I work in the legal department here in Brazil.
J
What do you do exactly in the legal department?
S
I am a corporate lawyer. I am responsible for contracts and mergers and acquisitions.
J
That sounds very interesting. How long have you been at Telefonica?
S
I have worked here for several years. I currently deal with international agreements.
J
We should definitely talk more. I deal with contracts for the UK operations.
S
I would love to discuss that. Could we schedule a meeting this week?
Comprehension

About the Dialogue

1. Where is James Wilson from?
The London office.
2. What does James deal with?
Contracts for the UK operations.
3. What does Simone suggest at the end?
Scheduling a meeting this week.
Listening

Meeting Opening

Listen to the opening of an international team meeting.

0:000:00
Speed
1. What does the speaker ask everyone to do?
Introduce themselves briefly: name, role, and office.
2. Which cities are mentioned?
London, Munich, and Sao Paulo.
Comprehension

About the Meeting

1. What will they discuss after introductions?
The agenda for the quarter.
2. How many offices are joining?
Three: London, Munich, and Sao Paulo.
3. What information should each person share?
Their name, role, and office.
Practice

Quick Challenge

0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
A colleague from the London office asks: "What do you do?" Introduce yourself professionally.
"I am a corporate lawyer at Telefonica. I am responsible for contracts and M and A."
Detective

Spot the Error

"She work at Telefonica."
"She works at Telefonica."
"I am responsible of contracts."
"I am responsible for contracts."
"Does she negotiates every day?"
"Does she negotiate every day?"
"He deal with international clients."
"He deals with international clients."
"Let me to introduce myself."
"Let me introduce myself."
0 / 5 errors found
Speaking

Oral Drilling

1. A colleague asks your name and role. What do you say?
"Let me introduce myself. I am Simone Marques from the legal department."
2. Someone asks what you are responsible for. Respond.
"I am responsible for contracts and mergers and acquisitions."
3. A partner asks what you currently work on. Answer.
"I currently deal with international agreements."
4. Someone asks which countries you work with. Respond.
"I deal with contracts between our offices in Brazil, the UK, and Germany."
5. You want to schedule a follow-up. What do you say?
"I would love to discuss this further. Could we schedule a meeting?"
6. Describe your experience in one sentence.
"I have worked in corporate law for over twenty years."
Build

Sentence Building

works / She / at / Telefonica / department / the / in / legal
"She works in the legal department at Telefonica."
responsible / am / I / contracts / for / international
"I am responsible for international contracts."
negotiate / Does / she / with / partners / terms / ?
"Does she negotiate terms with partners?"
currently / We / a / deal / working / new / on / are
"We are currently working on a new deal."
colleague / My / Germany / the / from / will / call / join
"My colleague from Germany will join the call."
Your Turn

Role-Play: Office Meeting

You are at a Telefonica international meeting. James Wilson from London approaches you. Introduce yourself, explain your role, and mention what you are currently working on.
introducecorporateresponsiblecurrentlycontracts
Role-Play

Role-Play: Video Conference

You are on a video call with a new colleague from Munich. They ask about your work. Explain your role, what you deal with, and suggest scheduling a follow-up meeting.
dealagreementmeeting
Free Practice

Role-Play: Networking

You are at a corporate dinner with international colleagues. A lawyer from another company asks about your career. Have a free conversation. No keywords, no script. Just talk!

Use everything you learned today: your name, role, responsibilities, experience, and colleagues.

Reflection

Record Yourself

Introduce yourself as if you are at a Telefonica international meeting. Include: your name, your role, what you are responsible for, and what you currently work on.

Click to start recording. Click again to stop.

Error Correction

Let's Fix Together

Teacher: type the errors from the role-plays below. Discuss corrections together.

Click here to type errors...
Wrap-Up

Contracts Badge Earned!

You can now introduce yourself confidently at any international meeting, Simone.

Key Phrases

5 Key Phrases

1
"Let me introduce myself. I am Simone Marques."
2
"I am a corporate lawyer at Telefonica."
3
"I am responsible for contracts and M&A."
4
"I currently deal with international agreements."
5
"Could we schedule a meeting this week?"
Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can introduce myself professionally in English.
I know 10 corporate and legal vocabulary words.
I can use Present Simple for facts and routines.
I can explain my role and responsibilities at Telefonica.
I can have a professional conversation with a colleague.
I can understand a professional introduction in English.
I can describe what I currently work on.
I feel more confident about speaking English at work.
Lesson Complete

Day 1 -- Complete.

Next lesson: My Daily Routine -- Work at Telefonica

"Forever Intermediate" -- ends here.

T
LESSON 1
